question: What are the AxCent PCB mount drive specifications?
expected_section: Products and System Requirements / Analog PWM Servo Drive Basics
expected_answer_contains: AxCent mount drives (AZB series) single-
full_expected_answer: AxCent PCB mount drives (AZB series) are single- or three-axis analog PWM servo drives with power ratings from 6A/8V (AZB6A8) to 60A/8V (AZB60A8) continuous, using pulse-width modulation at a fixed switching frequency for current control — the PWM on-time is proportional to commanded current. They accept a +/-10V analog command input (100k ohm input impedance), operate from DC supply voltages up to 90V, and mount on the optional MC1XAZ01-HR carrier board which provides screw terminals and interface circuits. AZB models support brushed or brushless motors; the AZBD variant adds a deadband adjustment for brushed voice-coil applications. The AZBH variant is optimized for Hall-only velocity control, while the AZBE variant supports encoder-based position control. All models include a +/-10V command input and a separate Enable input (active high, 3.3–5V compatible). question: How do I wire power and motor connections for an analog servo drive?
expected_section: Integration in the Servo System / Wiring
expected_answer_contains: Install European-approved over-current protective device (circuit
full_expected_answer: Install a European-approved over-current protective device (circuit breaker or fuse) rated for the AC input line current in series with the AC supply before the drive or power supply. Wire sizing must comply with LVD requirements: use minimum 14 AWG for 30A-rated drives (B30A40) and 10 AWG for 100A models (B100A400AC). Keep DC power wires (HV+ and HV-) as short as possible; if they exceed 5 feet, add a 1000µF bypass capacitor near the drive's power input terminals. Use shielded cable for motor connections, with the shield connected to earth ground at the drive end only. For the full guide, download the Classic Analog Drives Hardware Installation Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What are the minimum inductance requirements for analog drives?
expected_answer_contains: servo drives produce high-frequency pulsed voltage
full_expected_answer: PWM servo drives produce a high-frequency pulsed voltage output (typically 20–100 kHz switching frequency) that requires a minimum phase-to-phase motor inductance to limit ripple current. For brushless motors, the minimum inductance is typically 500µH phase-to-phase for drives switching at 20 kHz; lower switching frequencies require higher minimum inductance proportionally. If the motor's inductance is too low, add external series inductors (line reactors) in series with each motor phase lead — size them to bring the total phase inductance to the minimum required value. Failure to meet minimum inductance can cause excessive current ripple, overheating, and potential drive damage. For the full guide, download the Classic Analog Drives Hardware Installation Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What command input options are available on analog drives?
expected_section: Products and System Requirements / Command Inputs
expected_answer_contains: Classic analog drives series) accept differential
full_expected_answer: Classic analog drives (B series) accept a differential +/-10V analog command signal on the CMD+ and CMD- input pins (100k ohm input impedance); this signal commands current, velocity, or position depending on the selected control mode. Full-scale command scaling (e.g., 10V = peak current, or 10V = max velocity) is set via the GAIN potentiometer or DIP switch current-select bits. An input OFFSET potentiometer trims any DC offset on the command signal. Some models also accept a step-and-direction or PWM command input — check the specific drive datasheet for availability. The Enable (inhibit) input must be pulled to logic high (5V) to allow drive output; leaving it unconnected will prevent the drive from operating. For the full guide, download the Classic Analog Drives Hardware Installation Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

After clicking Connect, the drive's workspace opens and the current drive configuration is uploaded automatically. Ensure the drive's Interface Access Control is set to allow full read/write access from the selected channel; if another channel has exclusive access, ACE will connect in read-only mode. Install the USB driver from the ACE installation package if connecting via USB for the first time. The motor rated speed (RPM) and back-EMF constant (Ke, V/KRPM) are required for AC induction and stepper configurations. Save parameters to EEPROM using the Save to Drive button after completing all motor settings. question: How do I set up commutation sensors (Halls) in ACE?
expected_answer_contains: under Configuring Drive Commutation, enable Hall
full_expected_answer: In ACE under Configuring the Drive > Commutation, enable Hall sensors and select the Hall phasing: 60-degree or 120-degree spacing depending on the motor specification (120-degree is most common for BLDC motors; use 60-degree for motors that specify it). The Hall State Sequence display shows the live Hall A/B/C states as the motor rotates — verify the sequence advances through all six valid states without invalid transitions (all-zeros or all-ones indicate a wiring fault). If the motor runs roughly or in the wrong direction, swap any two motor phase wires and re-run AutoCommutation to re-detect the commutation offset angle. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I configure digital inputs and outputs in ACE?
expected_answer_contains: under Configuring Drive Inputs/Outputs, each programmable
full_expected_answer: In ACE under Configuring the Drive > Inputs/Outputs, each programmable digital input (PDI1–PDI7 on FlexPro) can be assigned a function from a drop-down list: Motion Enable, Positive/Negative Limit Switch, Home Switch, Index Trigger, Capture Input, Fault Reset, Gain Set Select, or user-defined. Each input can be configured as active-high or active-low. Digital outputs (PDO1–PDO4) can be assigned to indicate In-Position, Motion Active, Drive Fault, At Speed, or custom conditions. The I/O Diagnostics tab shows the real-time state of all inputs and outputs as green (active) or grey (inactive) indicators — use this to verify wiring before running motion. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I configure the external shunt resistor in ACE?
expected_answer_contains: under Configuring Drive Limits Shunt, enable
full_expected_answer: In ACE under Configuring the Drive > Limits > Shunt, enable the external shunt and set the Shunt Resistance value (ohms) and Shunt Power rating (watts) to match the external resistor installed on the drive's SHUNT output terminals. When DC bus voltage exceeds the shunt threshold (typically 75V for 400V-class drives), the drive activates the shunt output for an initial 10ms pulse to bleed excess regenerated energy; the duty cycle then scales with bus voltage. Minimum shunt resistance values by power module: 030A400 = 20 ohm min, 060A400 = 10 ohm min, 100A400 = 10 ohm min. Always verify the shunt resistor's wattage can handle peak regenerative energy; under-rated resistors can overheat and fail. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I run the current loop auto-tune in ACE?
expected_section: Tuning and Commutation / Current Loop Auto-Tuning
expected_answer_contains: under Tuning Commutation Current Loop, click
full_expected_answer: In ACE under Tuning and Commutation > Current Loop, click Auto-Tune to launch the current loop auto-tuning wizard; the drive must be enabled and the motor connected with proper feedback configured. The auto-tune injects a test current signal, measures the motor's electrical time constant (L/R), and calculates optimal proportional (Kp) and integral (Ki) current loop gains. The result is displayed as Current Loop Bandwidth in Hz — a typical target is 1–3 kHz for most servo applications. After auto-tune completes, verify the step response on the oscilloscope (Channel 2 set to Current Measured) shows fast rise with minimal overshoot before saving parameters. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I manually tune the current loop in ACE?
expected_section: Tuning and Commutation / Current Loop Tuning
expected_answer_contains: manual current loop tuning ACE, open
full_expected_answer: For manual current loop tuning in ACE, open Tuning and Commutation > Current Loop and set the oscilloscope Channel 1 to Current Target and Channel 2 to Current Measured. Start with the integral gain (Ki) at zero and increase proportional gain (Kp) until the measured current rises quickly and tracks the target step without sustained oscillation, then back off 20%. Next, increase Ki gradually until steady-state current error is eliminated without introducing overshoot; typical starting values are Kp = 100–300 and Ki = 10–50 (in drive-specific units — see the manual for scaling). Disable the drive before changing gain values if the motor exhibits unstable behavior, and use small increments when tuning. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I run AutoCommutation in ACE?
expected_answer_contains: Before running AutoCommutation ACE, verify motor
full_expected_answer: Before running AutoCommutation in ACE, verify the motor type, pole pairs, and encoder resolution are correctly set in Motor > Parameters, and ensure the current loop is tuned (auto or manual). The routine runs a Phase Detect step (injects DC current to align the rotor to a known position) followed by a slow rotation to measure the commutation offset angle and encoder/Hall polarity. The motor must be free to rotate 2–3 mechanical turns; remove any hard mechanical stops. If AutoCommutation succeeds, the Commutation Offset angle (in degrees electrical) and feedback polarity flags are stored automatically — verify operation with a short jog before finalizing. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: What should I do if Phase Detect fails during AutoCommutation?
expected_answer_contains: Phase Detect fails ACE, first check
full_expected_answer: If Phase Detect fails in ACE, first check that the continuous current limit is set to at least 25% of peak current — a limit that is too low prevents the routine from generating sufficient torque to align the rotor. Also verify the motor wiring: all three phase wires must be connected (for brushless) and there must be no mechanical brake or hard stop preventing rotor movement. If the routine fails after two automatic retries, try manually increasing the Phase Detect Current (in Tuning > Commutation settings) by 25% increments. For encoder-only motors without Halls, confirm the encoder Z-pulse (index) is present and correctly wired, as Phase Detect relies on finding the index. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I tune the position loop in ACE?
expected_section: Tuning and Commutation / Position Loop Tuning
expected_answer_contains: ACE, tune position loop after current
full_expected_answer: In ACE, tune the position loop after the current and velocity loops are stable. Use the Position Loop Auto-Tuning wizard or tune manually under Tuning and Commutation > Position Loop: set the oscilloscope Channel 1 to Position Target and Channel 2 to Position Measured, then command a profiled move. Increase proportional gain (Kp) until following error during motion is minimized, then add integral gain (Ki) to eliminate steady-state position error at rest. Add derivative gain (Kd) or velocity feedforward only if needed to reduce overshoot. The In-Position window and In-Position Time parameters (under Motion Tasks) define when the drive asserts the In-Position output — set these to appropriate values for your application tolerance. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I configure homing parameters in ACE?
expected_answer_contains: under Motion Tasks Homing, configure: Fast
full_expected_answer: In ACE under Motion Tasks > Homing, configure: Fast Velocity (speed in RPM or user units for the initial switch-search phase), Slow Velocity (reduced speed for index pulse or home-switch-edge detection), Acceleration and Deceleration for both phases, and the Home Offset (units in encoder counts applied after the home position is found). Select the Homing Method that matches your hardware: common options include Homing on negative limit switch with index pulse (Method 1), positive limit switch with index (Method 2), or home switch only without index (Method 17/18). The homing sequence is initiated from the Motion Panel, a digital input trigger, or a network command (object 6098h). For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I set up and run motion indexes in ACE?
expected_answer_contains: under Motion Tasks Motion Tasks, create
full_expected_answer: In ACE under Motion Tasks > Motion Tasks, create Index entries by specifying: Index Type (Absolute or Relative), Target Position (in user units or encoder counts), Maximum Velocity, Acceleration, Deceleration, and a Dwell time after the move completes. Up to 128 indexes can be stored in the drive's motion engine. Trigger an index from the Motion Panel (for testing), assign a digital input to trigger a specific index number on a rising edge, or initiate via network command (CANopen: write to object 607Ah and set bit 4 of the controlword). Link indexes into sequences using Sequence Tasks to create automated multi-step motion programs. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I use PVT (Position-Velocity-Time) motion profiles in ACE?
expected_answer_contains: under Motion Tasks PVT, build table
full_expected_answer: In ACE under Motion Tasks > PVT, build a PVT table by defining rows of (Time interval in ms, Velocity at endpoint in RPM or user units, Delta Position in user units). The drive uses cubic spline interpolation between points to produce smooth jerk-limited motion. A PVT buffer holds up to 255 points; ensure the host streams new points before the buffer empties using the buffer-level output object. I/O sync can be configured to assert a digital output at a specific PVT table step — useful for triggering external devices at precise trajectory points. PVT mode is initiated by setting the drive's Mode of Operation to PVT (mode 7), then sending Start PVT via the controlword. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I use the digital oscilloscope in ACE?
expected_answer_contains: digital oscilloscope (Tools Digital Oscilloscope) captures
full_expected_answer: The ACE digital oscilloscope (Tools > Digital Oscilloscope) captures up to 4 simultaneous channels of drive-internal signals at the servo loop sample rate (typically 4–16 kHz). Assign signals to each channel from the drop-down: common combinations are Channel 1 = Position Target + Channel 2 = Position Measured (to check following error), or Channel 1 = Current Target + Channel 2 = Current Measured (for current loop tuning). Set the Trigger source, level, and pre/post-trigger window, then click Arm — the scope captures automatically when the trigger condition is met. Use Time/Div and the zoom controls to inspect transients; export data as CSV via File > Export for external analysis. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I use the Motion Engine in ACE?
expected_answer_contains: Motion Engine drive's onboard task scheduler
full_expected_answer: The Motion Engine in ACE is the drive's onboard task scheduler for executing Indexes, Sequences, and Homing autonomously. States: Disabled (no motion possible), Ready (waiting for a trigger), Running (executing a task), and Error (fault during motion). Enable the Motion Engine by clicking the Enable button in the toolbar (or writing to the Motion Engine enable object over the network) — the drive must already be in Operation Enabled state. Trigger tasks by number from the Motion Panel for testing, via a digital input edge (assign Trigger Index N to a PDI), or via a network command. The Motion Engine can also be configured to auto-start a sequence on power-up via the Start-Up Task setting. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I download firmware to a FlexPro drive using ACE?
expected_answer_contains: ACE, File Download Firmware Firmware button
full_expected_answer: In ACE, go to File > Download Firmware (or the Firmware button in the toolbar). Use Browse Folder to point to the directory containing the firmware .bin file, or Browse File to select a specific file; firmware files are named by drive family and version (e.g., FlexPro_v2.xx.xx.bin). ACE downloads the firmware over the active communication channel — USB or RS485 recommended (Ethernet is supported but slower). Do NOT disconnect the drive or cut power during the download; if the process is interrupted, use the recovery mode documented in the manual to restore the drive. After download, ACE will prompt you to power-cycle the drive to activate the new firmware. For the full guide, download the ACE Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I connect DriveWare to a DigiFlex Performance drive?
expected_answer_contains: Launch DriveWare click Connection; select RS-232
full_expected_answer: Launch DriveWare and click New Connection; select RS-232 (for DP panel drives via DB-9 serial cable), RS-485/CANopen, or USB depending on the drive's available interface. For RS-232, set baud rate to 9600 (default) and COM port to match your PC's serial port; the default drive address is 63. After connecting, the Bridge Status indicator in the lower toolbar shows the drive state (red = fault, yellow = not enabled, green = enabled). Use the Waveform Generator (Tools menu) for open-loop testing and the Motion Panel to jog or trigger indexes. If the connection fails, verify the Interface Access Control setting in the drive is not locked to a different channel. question: How do I view drive hardware information in DriveWare?

full_expected_answer: In DriveWare, go to Help > Drive Information to open the Drive Information panel, which displays: firmware version (e.g., 5.x.x.x), control board revision, power module part number and current/voltage rating, drive serial number, and MAC address (for Ethernet-capable drives). This panel is useful when reporting issues to AMC support. Also accessible via the status bar at the bottom of the DriveWare window, which shows current drive state, active faults, and bus voltage in real time. question: How do I configure motor parameters in DriveWare?
expected_answer_contains: DriveWare, navigate Configuring Drive Motor select
full_expected_answer: In DriveWare, navigate to Configuring the Drive > Motor and select Motor Type: Brushed DC, Brushless (BLDC), Stepper, or AC Induction. For brushless motors set the number of pole pairs (motor poles ÷ 2) and encoder lines-per-revolution. Set Continuous Current Limit (max sustained output in Amps) and Peak Current Limit (max short-term current); the peak-to-continuous ratio determines the foldback time constant. For steppers, also set the number of steps per revolution and microstepping resolution. Click Save to EEPROM (or use File > Save Parameters) after completing configuration. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I configure velocity feedback in DriveWare?
expected_answer_contains: DriveWare under Configuring Drive Velocity Feedback,
full_expected_answer: In DriveWare under Configuring the Drive > Velocity Feedback, select the velocity source: Motor Encoder Derived (calculates velocity from encoder count differentiation — most accurate for high-resolution encoders), Hall Velocity (derives velocity from Hall transition timing — useful when no encoder is present), or Tachometer (+/-10V analog tachometer input). Set the Velocity Feedback Filter cutoff frequency to reduce noise; a typical starting value is 500–2000 Hz. For encoder-derived velocity, also set the Velocity Feedback Resolution (post-quadrature encoder counts used for velocity calculation). Hall-derived velocity is less accurate at low speeds; switch to encoder-derived when encoders are available. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I run AutoCommutation in DriveWare?
expected_answer_contains: DriveWare, navigate Configuring Drive Commutation click
full_expected_answer: In DriveWare, navigate to Configuring the Drive > Commutation and click AutoCommutation. The routine performs Phase Detect (locks rotor with DC current) then slowly rotates the motor to measure the commutation angle offset and verify encoder/Hall polarity. Prerequisites: motor parameters (pole pairs, encoder resolution) must be set, current loop must be tuned, and the motor must rotate freely for 2–3 turns without mechanical stops. If the routine succeeds, it writes the Commutation Offset angle and Encoder Polarity flags to EEPROM automatically. Verify with a jog move: smooth, controllable rotation at commanded velocity indicates correct commutation; erratic motion or excessive current suggests a phase wiring issue. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I configure digital input functions in DriveWare?
expected_answer_contains: DriveWare under Configuring Drive Digital I/O,
full_expected_answer: In DriveWare under Configuring the Drive > Digital I/O, each digital input (DI1–DI8 depending on drive model) has a Function drop-down: Motion Enable (must be asserted to enable the drive bridge), Set Position (latches current position to a preset value on rising edge), Positive/Negative Limit Switch, Home Switch, Index Trigger, Fault Reset, or Capture. Set Polarity (active-high or active-low) for each input to match hardware wiring. Digital outputs (DO1–DO4) can indicate: In-Position, Motion Active, At Speed, Fault, or custom program-controlled states. The I/O Status display shows live input/output states for wiring verification. question: What are the minimum shunt resistance values for DigiFlex drives?
expected_answer_contains: Minimum external shunt resistance values DigiFlex
full_expected_answer: Minimum external shunt resistance values by DigiFlex power module (to prevent overcurrent damage to the shunt FET): 015A400 = no external shunt available/N/A; 030A400 = 20 ohm minimum; 060A400 = 10 ohm minimum; 100A400 = 10 ohm minimum. For 800V-class power modules, consult the factory for shunt requirements. In DriveWare under Configuring the Drive > Shunt, enter the actual shunt resistance and rated wattage; the drive uses these values to compute the shunt duty-cycle limit to prevent overheating. The shunt activates when DC bus voltage exceeds the configured shunt threshold (nominally 75V for 400V-class drives). question: How do I tune the current loop in DriveWare?
expected_section: Tuning and Commutation / Current Loop Tuning
expected_answer_contains: DriveWare under Tuning Current Loop, follow
full_expected_answer: In DriveWare under Tuning > Current Loop, follow the 4-step procedure: Step 1 — set Kp (proportional gain) starting from a low value (e.g., 50); Step 2 — enable the drive and use the Waveform Generator to command a current step, observe on the oscilloscope (Channel 1 = Current Target, Channel 2 = Current Measured); Step 3 — increase Kp until current rise is fast with minimal ringing, then back off 10–20%; Step 4 — add Ki (integral gain) to eliminate steady-state error without causing oscillation. For AC Induction motors, also complete Step 7: tune the Flux Reference loop (Kp-flux, Ki-flux) using the magnetizing current response. Save parameters after tuning. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I tune the velocity loop in DriveWare?
expected_section: Tuning and Commutation / Velocity Loop Tuning
expected_answer_contains: DriveWare under Tuning Velocity Loop, oscilloscope
full_expected_answer: In DriveWare under Tuning > Velocity Loop, set the oscilloscope to Channel 1 = Velocity Target and Channel 2 = Velocity Measured. Command a velocity step from the Motion Panel and observe the response. Increase Kp (proportional gain) until the velocity follows the target quickly with minimal overshoot (aim for <10% overshoot), then add Ki (integral gain) in small increments to eliminate steady-state velocity error at constant speed. Add Kd (derivative gain) or Acceleration Feedforward only if needed to reduce velocity lag during ramps. A velocity loop bandwidth of 50–200 Hz is typical for most servo systems. Ensure the current loop bandwidth is at least 5–10x the velocity loop bandwidth. For the full guide, download the DriveWare Software User Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I run motor commutation in DriveWare?

Click AutoCommutation to launch: the routine injects DC current, aligns the rotor, then rotates it slowly to measure the electrical commutation offset angle. After completion, the Commutation Offset value (degrees electrical) is displayed and saved to EEPROM. Test with a jog command; consistent smooth rotation confirms correct commutation. question: How do I set up homing in DriveWare?

After a successful home, the drive's position counter is set to the Home Offset value and the Homed status bit (statusword bit 12) is set. question: What are the EtherCAT drive control state machine states?
expected_answer_contains: drive state machine states controlled Controlword
full_expected_answer: The CiA 402 drive state machine has 8 states controlled via the Controlword (object 6040h) and monitored via the Statusword (object 6041h): Not Ready to Switch On (power-on initialization, not user-accessible), Switch On Disabled (power bridge disabled, drive idle), Ready to Switch On (fault-free, waiting for Switch On command), Switched On (bridge enabled, current loops active, waiting for Enable Operation), Operation Enabled (full servo control active, motor responds to commands), Quick Stop Active (deceleration in progress after Quick Stop command), Fault Reaction Active (fault detected, executing fault reaction), and Fault (fault state, requires Fault Reset command to clear). The standard enable sequence writes Controlword = 6 (Ready to Switch On), then 7 (Switch On), then 15 (Enable Operation). For the full guide, download the FlexPro EtherCAT Communication Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What does the Operation Enabled state mean?
expected_answer_contains: Operation Enabled (Statusword bits 0x37, i.e.,
full_expected_answer: Operation Enabled (Statusword bits 0–5 = 0x37, i.e., bits: Ready=1, Switched On=1, Operation Enabled=1, Fault=0, Voltage Enabled=1, Quick Stop=1) is the normal run state: the power bridge is energized, all control loops (current, velocity, position) are active, and the drive executes commands received via PDO or SDO. To enter this state, the host writes Controlword = 15 (0x000F) while in Switched On state. To leave gracefully, write Controlword = 7 (disable operation), then 6 (switch off) to return to Switch On Disabled. Any fault condition automatically transitions the drive to Fault Reaction Active then Fault; write Controlword bit 7 = 1 (Fault Reset) to return to Switch On Disabled after clearing the fault condition. For the full guide, download the FlexPro EtherCAT Communication Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I troubleshoot a red LED on an analog drive?
expected_answer_contains: indicates fault; follow 9-step isolation procedure
full_expected_answer: A red LED indicates a fault; follow the 9-step isolation procedure in App Note 009. Common causes include invalid Hall sensor states (valid 60° states: 001,010,011,100,101,110; all others invalid), over-temperature (>65°C on thermistor), over/under voltage, or a motor short. Disable the drive, check Hall wiring phasing (60° vs 120° DIP switch), verify motor wiring continuity, then re-enable. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 009 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What causes I²t foldback on an analog drive?
expected_answer_contains: foldback (current foldback) activates when motor
full_expected_answer: I²t foldback (current foldback) activates when the motor draws continuous current above the set continuous current limit for too long, preventing winding overheating. The drive accumulates an internal I²t counter; when it reaches 100%, output current is folded back to the continuous limit. Reduce peak demand, increase continuous current rating, or improve thermal conditions. For the full guide, download the AMC Hardware Manual — Analog Drives from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: How do I set the continuous current limit on an analog drive?
expected_answer_contains: Turn CURR LIMIT potentiometer using formula:
full_expected_answer: Turn the CURR LIMIT potentiometer using the formula: turns from full CCW = (Icont / Imax) × 12 + 1, where the pot has 12 active turns plus 1 inactive turn at each end (14 total). Example: B25A20 (5A continuous, 12.5A peak) = (5/12.5) × 12 + 1 = 5.8 turns ≈ 6 turns. Example: B60A40 (10A/15A) = (10/15) × 12 + 1 = 9 turns. The CURRENT SCALING and CONT. CURR. REDUCTION switches further adjust output scaling. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 011 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I tune the current loop on an analog drive?
expected_answer_contains: Current loop tuning uses switches (proportional
full_expected_answer: Current loop tuning uses DIP switches SW3 (proportional gain), SW4 (integral gain), and SW7 (bandwidth selection). Start with SW7 in low-bandwidth position, then increase SW3 (proportional) until oscillation occurs, then back off one position. Increase SW4 (integrator capacitor) incrementally to reduce steady-state error. S-Series drives require a triple-loop tuning approach. Mill Max P/N 8427 pin receptacles can be used for temporary DIP switch override during tuning. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 015 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I wire a brushed motor to a brushless amplifier?
expected_answer_contains: brushed motor brushless amplifier, disconnect Hall
full_expected_answer: To run a brushed motor on a brushless amplifier, disconnect all Hall sensor inputs and set the 60° phasing DIP switch to OFF (toward the outside of the case). Connect Motor A to the negative terminal and Motor B to the positive terminal; leave Motor C unconnected. This configures the drive for single-phase (brush) commutation. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 007 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What are the Hall sensor valid states for 60° and 120° phasing?
expected_answer_contains: phasing, valid Hall states (H1, are:
full_expected_answer: For 60° phasing, valid Hall states (H1, H2, H3) are: 001, 010, 011, 100, 101, 110 — states 000 and 111 are invalid faults. For 120° phasing the same six states are valid but phase assignments differ. An invalid Hall state (000 or 111) will cause a red LED fault and the drive will not commutate. Verify wiring polarity and sensor phasing before enabling the bridge. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 009 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What network options are available for AMC drives?
expected_answer_contains: drives support: CANopen Mbit/s, nodes, ~130
full_expected_answer: AMC drives support: CANopen (1 Mbit/s, 128 nodes, ~130 µs message time), RS232 (115.2 kbit/s, 1 node, ~1.45 ms), RS485 2-wire or 4-wire (921.6 kbit/s, 32 nodes, ~324 µs), EtherCAT (2×100 Mbit/s, 65535 nodes, ~100 µs), and POWERLINK (100 Mbit/s, 240 nodes). EtherCAT has the highest node count and lowest latency. Choose based on master controller support and required update rate. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 001 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I connect a DZ PCB drive via RS485 using the MC1XDZ adapter?
expected_answer_contains: connect DZRALTE RS485 using MC1XDZ interface
full_expected_answer: To connect a DZRALTE via RS485 using the MC1XDZ interface card, install the J3 jumper on the DZRALTE and move JF2 and JF3 jumpers on the MC1XDZ from pins 1-2 to pins 2-3. Default communication settings are 9600 baud and address 63. Note that RS232 and RS485 parameters are stored independently in NVM and must be configured separately for each interface. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 002 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What are the design requirements for the DZ PCB drive interface card?
expected_answer_contains: interface card address lines must exceed
full_expected_answer: The DZ interface card address lines must not exceed 3V; use a 500 kΩ voltage divider to meet this limit. The DZC variant requires an external CAN transceiver — it is not built in. High-speed digital inputs use a 250 Ω + 100 pF RC filter plus 10 kΩ pull-up and 20 kΩ R2 for single-ended conversion. Analog inputs use a 2 kΩ + 6.7 kΩ divider network. Maximum current per HV pin is 3A, and a separate 5 VDC ±5% logic supply is required. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 008 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I send RS485 serial commands to a FlexPro drive?
expected_answer_contains: RS485 messages CRC-16-CCITT XModem protocol. Each
full_expected_answer: RS485 messages use the CRC-16-CCITT XModem protocol. Each message header contains: SOF byte A5h, node address, control byte, object index (e.g., 2047h for PVT points, 2048h for command source, 2001h for bridge enable), sub-index, data words, and 2-byte CRC. A 7-point trapezoidal PVT profile example is included in App Note 006. A stop point is defined by velocity = 0 and time = 0. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 006 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I use Docklight to communicate with an AMC drive over serial?
expected_answer_contains: Configure Docklight 9600 baud (default setting)
full_expected_answer: Configure Docklight to 9600 baud (default AMC setting) and compose messages using the AMC serial protocol: SOF byte A5h, address byte, control byte, object index, sub-index offset, data word(s), and 2-byte CRC-16-CCITT XModem. To enable the bridge, send the appropriate index 45h interface input command. Example TX/RX hex messages for bridge enable are provided in App Note 036. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 036 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I configure PVT motion profiles over RS485?
expected_answer_contains: (Position-Velocity-Time) profiles loaded RS485 using object
full_expected_answer: PVT (Position-Velocity-Time) profiles are loaded via RS485 using object index 2047h for each PVT point. Set command source via object 2048h and enable the bridge via object 2001h. The drive uses a 7th-order polynomial interpolator with a 14-point buffer. A stop point is always defined as velocity = 0 and time = 0 at the end of the profile. A 7-point trapezoidal example with exact hex messages is in App Note 006. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 006 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I set up sequencing on an AMC drive?
expected_answer_contains: Motion Engine supports sequences, each with
full_expected_answer: The Motion Engine supports up to 16 sequences, each with up to 5 step categories (move, wait, set I/O, jump, stop). For power-up auto-start, set Bridge Status = Software Enable, Motion Engine = Enable, and Default Mode = Sequencer in the drive configuration. Assign digital I/O lines using Motion Select 0–3 bits to trigger sequences externally. Use breakpoints in the sequencer for step-by-step debugging. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 035 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I enable the Motion Engine at power-up?
expected_answer_contains: enable Motion Engine power-up, write object
full_expected_answer: To enable the Motion Engine at power-up, write to object 20C8.01h (Modbus register 40016). Bit 4 = enable Motion Engine; bits 0–2 = motion type; bits 3–4 = selection source; bits 5–8 = startup index; bits 9–15 = auto-initiate settings. Alternatively, use object 2008.01h bit 4. When the Motion Engine is enabled, it takes exclusive control of the drive command source. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 027 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I troubleshoot USB and RS232 connection issues in ACE software?
expected_answer_contains: Default address multi-axis addresses drive does
full_expected_answer: Default USB address is 63; for multi-axis use addresses 62, 61, 60. If the drive does not appear, re-enumerate via Windows Device Manager (uninstall device, reconnect USB). For RS232 use a straight (not null-modem) cable. Recommended USB-to-serial adapters: ATEN USB to Serial 1.5 or B&B Electronics USOPTL4/USO9ML2-LS. Perform a loopback test by connecting DB-9 pins 2+3 and 7+8 (tie 1+4+6) to verify adapter function. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 050 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I configure the Programmable Limit Switch (PLS)?
expected_answer_contains: provides independent outputs configurable position-based time-based
full_expected_answer: The PLS provides two independent outputs configurable for position-based or time-based pulse modes. In rotary mode with rollover, the condition Lower Edge + Repeat Delta > Upper Edge must be met. Configure repeat count and conveyor belt parameters as needed. Use ACE software to assign PLS outputs to digital output channels and set the edge positions and pulse widths. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 034 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What ferrite cores should I use for EMI filtering on AMC drives?
expected_answer_contains: recommends Fair-Rite ferrite cores suppression. Noise
full_expected_answer: AMC recommends Fair-Rite and TDK ferrite cores for EMI suppression. Noise typically appears at 30–40 MHz. Choose the ferrite by wire gauge: AWG 28–16 uses smaller cores, AWG 20–14 uses medium cores, AWG 12–8 and 10–6 use larger cores. Each ferrite in App Note 023 is listed with impedance values at 10 MHz, 25 MHz, and 100 MHz. Install ferrites on motor power leads and signal cables near the drive. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 023 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I set up the MACC network controller?
expected_answer_contains: MACC default address 192.168.100.50; configure your
full_expected_answer: The MACC default IP address is 192.168.100.50; configure your PC to IP 192.168.100.240, subnet 255.255.255.0. Verify connectivity with a ping test before attempting communication. Use the C&M compiler with target MAL-MACC to compile and download motion programs. Ensure the Ethernet cable is connected before powering the MACC. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 047 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I download a Click&Move application to a drive?
expected_answer_contains: Click&Move, select target PLA-AMC Programmable Servo
full_expected_answer: In Click&Move, select target PLA-AMC Programmable Servo Drive Platform A. Compile the application to a .dff file (CMApplication.dff) to burn to NVM, or a .lff file to run via Ethernet without NVM write. Use the DriveWare Firmware Download procedure to transfer the file. To remove a Click&Move application, download clearapp.dff to the drive. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 049 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What are the STO wiring requirements for DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT drives?
expected_answer_contains: wiring DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT uses: STO-1,
full_expected_answer: STO wiring on the DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT uses: pin 4 = STO-1, pin 6 = STO-2, pin 3 = STO-1 return, pin 5 = STO-2 return, pin 1 = STO output signal. Both STO-1 and STO-2 must be energized (24V) for the drive to operate; removing either channel disables torque output (SIL 3, Cat 4 / PL e). A monthly proof test is required per IEC 62061 to verify both channels operate independently. For the full guide, download the AMC Hardware Manual — DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What are the I/O timing parameters for the DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT drive?
expected_answer_contains: Digital outputs DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT isolated
full_expected_answer: Digital outputs on the DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT are isolated 24V bi-directional optical isolators. Active High outputs have a 1900 ms power cycle delay and 1800 ms reset delay. These delays must be accounted for in system startup sequencing. Inputs are also optically isolated and rated for 24V logic. For the full guide, download the AMC Hardware Manual — DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: How does thermal derating work on DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT drives?
expected_answer_contains: Thermal derating curves DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT
full_expected_answer: Thermal derating curves on the DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT are provided per model in the hardware manual on page 25. Each curve shows maximum continuous output current vs. ambient temperature. Above the rated ambient temperature, current capability decreases linearly. Ensure adequate airflow or heatsinking to stay within the derating curve for your ambient conditions. For the full guide, download the AMC Hardware Manual — DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What are the STO terminals on the FlexPro PCB drive?
expected_answer_contains: FlexPro drive connections terminals Operating temperature
full_expected_answer: The FlexPro PCB drive STO connections use terminals T1, T2, T3, and T4. Operating temperature range is 0–65°C ambient (storage: -40 to 85°C). PCB design requirements include 1 oz copper planes. Firmware versions prior to 1-2021-4 require an I2C expander; later firmware removed this dependency. Use star-point grounding on the PCB to minimize noise. For the full guide, download the AMC Hardware Manual — FlexPro PCB from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: How do I configure EtherCAT PDO assignments on a FlexPro drive?
expected_answer_contains: Default RPDO (master→drive) objects: 6040.00h (controlword),
full_expected_answer: Default RPDO (master→drive) objects: 6040.00h (controlword), 607A.00h (target position), 60FF.00h (target velocity), 6071.00h (target torque), 2001.03h (AMC control). Default TPDO (drive→master) objects: 6041.00h (statusword), 6064.00h (actual position), 606C.00h (actual velocity), 6077.00h (actual torque), 2023.01h (AMC status). Each PDO supports up to 16 words (256 bits). To modify, use the 3-step process: edit in DriveWare, update the ESI XML file, then write the EEPROM in TwinCAT. Objects 1600h/1A00h allow direct host writes. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 017 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I read the absolute encoder fault word over EtherCAT?
expected_answer_contains: absolute encoder fault word object 2027.04h.
full_expected_answer: The absolute encoder fault word is at object 2027.04h. For Hiperface encoders, fault codes range from 00h (no fault) through 28h (various sensor errors). For EnDat encoders, the fault word is bit-mapped with each bit indicating a specific encoder alarm. Read this object via SDO in the CANopen-over-EtherCAT (CoE) protocol to diagnose encoder issues. For the full guide, download the AMC Communication Manual — EtherCAT from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What is the CANopen EMCY message structure?
expected_answer_contains: CANopen Emergency (EMCY) messages COB-ID Node-ID.
full_expected_answer: CANopen Emergency (EMCY) messages use COB-ID = 80h + Node-ID. The 8-byte message contains: bytes 0-1 = error code (00h=no error, 01h=generic error, 02h=current, 80h–FFh = manufacturer-specific with bit definitions), byte 2 = error register, bytes 3-7 = manufacturer-specific error field. EMCY messages are transmitted when a fault occurs and again when the fault clears. For the full guide, download the AMC Communication Manual — CANopen from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I read the absolute encoder fault word over CANopen?
expected_answer_contains: absolute encoder fault word CANopen object
full_expected_answer: The absolute encoder fault word is at CANopen object 2027.04h. Hiperface encoder fault codes: 00h = no fault, 01h–28h indicate specific sensor errors. EnDat encoder faults are bit-mapped: each bit represents a distinct alarm condition. Access via SDO read using index 2027h, sub-index 04h. A non-zero value indicates an active encoder alarm. For the full guide, download the AMC Communication Manual — CANopen from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What is the Ethernet/IP CiA 402 state machine?
expected_answer_contains: drive state machine states: Ready Switch
full_expected_answer: The CiA 402 drive state machine has 7 states: Not Ready to Switch On, Switch On Disabled, Ready to Switch On, Switched On, Operation Enabled, Quick Stop Active, and Fault. Transitions are driven by controlword bits in the EtherNet/IP implicit I/O. From Fault state, issue a Fault Reset command (controlword bit 7 rising edge) to return to Switch On Disabled. Normal enable sequence: Switch On → Enable Operation. For the full guide, download the AMC Communication Manual — EtherNet/IP FlexPro from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What modes are available via Ethernet/IP instance 913?
expected_answer_contains: Ethernet/IP implicit assembly instance supports operating
full_expected_answer: Ethernet/IP implicit I/O assembly instance 913 supports 8 operating modes: position, velocity, torque, and additional AMC-specific modes. Mode selection is via the controlword in the RPDO. Each mode maps different process data to the assembly. Use the I/O configuration in your PLC to select instance 913 and map the correct data types for your application. For the full guide, download the AMC Communication Manual — EtherNet/IP FlexPro from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I configure the shunt resistor on a FlexPro drive?
expected_answer_contains: Minimum external shunt resistance values power
full_expected_answer: Minimum external shunt resistance values by power module: 015A400 = N/A (no shunt required), 030A400 = 20 Ω, 060A400 = 10 Ω, and larger modules have progressively lower minimums. Using a resistance below the minimum will damage the shunt transistor. Configure the shunt resistor value in DriveWare under the bus voltage parameters. For the full guide, download the AMC Software Manual — DriveWare from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: What is Integrator Decay and how do I configure it?
expected_answer_contains: Integrator Decay reduces accumulated integrator (I-term)
full_expected_answer: Integrator Decay reduces accumulated integrator (I-term) values when the axis is within a configurable Active Window. Set the decay rate from 0–100% in DriveWare. Velocity and position integrators both have independent decay settings. Use Active Window to define the position error threshold below which decay is active, preventing integrator wind-up at rest. For the full guide, download the AMC Software Manual — DriveWare from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How does the ACE software connect to a drive?
expected_answer_contains: ACE, Connect Drive window with default
full_expected_answer: In ACE, use the Connect To Drive window with default settings: RS232, address 63, baud rate 115200. For USB connections, the default address is also 63. If multiple drives are on the bus, assign unique addresses (62, 61, 60). For RS485 multi-drop, ensure each drive has a unique address and the baud rate matches all nodes. For the full guide, download the AMC Software Manual — ACE from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I configure the velocity feedback filter in ACE?
expected_answer_contains: velocity feedback filter adjustable cutoff frequency
full_expected_answer: The velocity feedback filter in ACE has an adjustable cutoff frequency range of 50–1500 Hz. Lower values reduce noise but increase phase lag; higher values provide faster response with more noise. Set the filter in the Velocity Loop tuning panel. For systems with high-inertia loads, start at 200–300 Hz and adjust based on stability. For the full guide, download the AMC Software Manual — ACE from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How does current loop auto-tune work in ACE?
expected_answer_contains: ACE's current loop auto-tune uses Identify
full_expected_answer: ACE's current loop auto-tune uses the Identify Model function to automatically measure motor electrical parameters (resistance, inductance) and compute optimal proportional and integral current loop gains. Run Identify Model with the motor stationary and connected. The function injects test signals and calculates gains; review and accept the computed values. For the full guide, download the AMC Software Manual — ACE from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: What is the biquad filter implementation in ACE?
expected_answer_contains: implements biquad filter Direct Form which
full_expected_answer: ACE implements the biquad filter in Direct Form II, which provides numerical stability for high-Q filters. Configure the filter with b0, b1, b2 (numerator) and a1, a2 (denominator) coefficients, or use the graphical frequency response editor. Biquad filters can be used for notch filtering resonances or as low-pass/high-pass elements in the velocity or position loop. For the full guide, download the AMC Software Manual — ACE from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I configure Hall sensor phasing on a DigiFlex PCB CANopen drive?
expected_answer_contains: DigiFlex CANopen drive supports both 120°
full_expected_answer: The DigiFlex PCB CANopen drive supports both 60° and 120° Hall phasing. The commutation table for both valid states (H1, H2, H3 = 001 through 110) is in the hardware manual. Select RS485 or RS232 via the onboard selection jumper. Logic supply must be 5 VDC ±5%. Firmware can be updated via the RS232 interface using the firmware update procedure in the manual. For the full guide, download the AMC Hardware Manual — DigiFlex PCB CANopen from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What safety rating does the AMC STO function achieve?
expected_answer_contains: AMC's (Safe Torque Off) function rated
full_expected_answer: AMC's STO (Safe Torque Off) function is rated SIL 3 per IEC 62061 and Category 4 / Performance Level e (PL e) per ISO 13849-1. It uses a dual-channel architecture with STO-1 and STO-2 as independent input channels; removing either channel independently disables torque output. A monthly proof test is required to demonstrate both channels operate independently. For the full guide, download the AMC Hardware Manual — DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What are the bus capacitance requirements for DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT drives?
expected_answer_contains: capacitance requirements DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT drive
full_expected_answer: Bus capacitance requirements for the DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT drive are specified in the hardware manual safety section (p.10). Minimum bus capacitance ensures stable operation and prevents voltage spikes during regeneration. Consult the per-model specification table for the minimum external capacitance value required for your supply voltage and load. For the full guide, download the AMC Hardware Manual — DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: How do I configure the drive for auto-start using the Motion Engine?
expected_answer_contains: auto-start motion power-up, object 20C8.01h bits
full_expected_answer: To auto-start a motion at power-up, set object 20C8.01h bits 9–15 (auto-initiate) in addition to bit 4 (enable) and bits 5–8 (startup index). In Modbus this is register 40016. Alternatively configure via object 2008.01h bit 4. Once set, the Motion Engine takes exclusive control of the drive and executes the configured startup index immediately upon enabling. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 027 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What are the valid Hall states for the DigiFlex PCB drive commutation?
expected_answer_contains: DigiFlex drive commutation table lists valid
full_expected_answer: The DigiFlex PCB drive commutation table lists valid Hall states (H1, H2, H3): 001, 010, 011, 100, 101, 110 for both 60° and 120° phasing. States 000 and 111 are invalid and will cause a commutation fault. Verify Hall sensor connections and phasing selection jumper position before enabling the drive. For the full guide, download the AMC Hardware Manual — DigiFlex PCB CANopen from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: How do I perform a serial loopback test for RS232?
expected_answer_contains: verify RS232 adapter, perform DB-9 loopback
full_expected_answer: To verify an RS232 adapter, perform a DB-9 loopback test: connect pin 2 to pin 3 (RX to TX), pin 7 to pin 8 (RTS to CTS), and tie pins 1, 4, and 6 together (DCD, DTR, DSR). Type characters in a terminal program and verify they echo back. Recommended USB-to-serial adapters: ATEN USB to Serial 1.5 or B&B Electronics USOPTL4/USO9ML2-LS. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 050 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How does the PLS rotary rollover mode work?
expected_answer_contains: rotary mode with rollover, output activates
full_expected_answer: In PLS rotary mode with rollover, the output activates when the axis position is between the Lower Edge and Upper Edge values and repeats after each Repeat Delta. The condition Lower Edge + Repeat Delta > Upper Edge must be satisfied for correct rollover behavior. Configure the repeat count for cyclic operation (e.g., conveyor belt applications). For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 034 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I configure breakpoints in the Motion Engine sequencer?
expected_answer_contains: Motion Engine sequencer supports breakpoints step-by-step
full_expected_answer: The Motion Engine sequencer supports breakpoints for step-by-step debugging. Set a breakpoint on any sequence step in ACE or DriveWare; the sequencer pauses at that step and waits for a manual continue command. Use this to verify I/O states, position, and velocity at each step of the sequence without running the full profile. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 035 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What is the difference between CMApplication.dff and CMApplication.lff?
expected_answer_contains: CMApplication.dff Click&Move application binary that burned
full_expected_answer: CMApplication.dff is the Click&Move application binary that is burned to drive NVM for persistent storage — it survives power cycles. CMApplication.lff is loaded into RAM via Ethernet and runs immediately without modifying NVM; it is lost on power cycle. Use .dff for production deployments and .lff for development/testing. To remove an application from NVM, download clearapp.dff. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 049 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What is the minimum shunt resistance for a 060A400 power module?
expected_answer_contains: minimum external shunt resistance 060A400 power
full_expected_answer: The minimum external shunt resistance for the 060A400 power module is 10 Ω. Using a lower resistance risks damaging the shunt transistor. The 030A400 module requires a minimum of 20 Ω, and the 015A400 module does not require an external shunt. Always configure the shunt value in DriveWare to match the installed resistor. For the full guide, download the AMC Software Manual — DriveWare from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: How do I configure digital I/O for sequencer triggering?
expected_answer_contains: Assign digital input lines Motion Select
full_expected_answer: Assign digital input lines to Motion Select 0, 1, 2, and 3 bits in the I/O configuration to select sequences externally. The 4-bit binary value on these inputs selects which of the 16 sequences runs. Configure the inputs as active high or active low to match your controller logic. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 035 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What are the CANopen object addresses for Motion Engine control?
expected_answer_contains: Motion Engine control uses object 20C8.01h
full_expected_answer: Motion Engine control uses object 20C8.01h (Modbus register 40016): bit 4 = enable, bits 0–2 = motion type, bits 3–4 = selection source, bits 5–8 = startup index, bits 9–15 = auto-initiate configuration. Object 2008.01h bit 4 is an alternative enable location. When the Motion Engine is enabled it takes exclusive command source control. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 027 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I connect an analog drive to a PC for configuration?

question: How does CANopen multi-axis addressing work?
