The EMCY message COB-ID = 80h + Node-ID, so each axis generates faults on a unique COB-ID. Default address for AMC drives is 63 (3Fh). For multi-axis RS485 networks, RS232 and RS485 NVM parameters are stored independently. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 002 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What is the FlexPro PCB temperature operating range?
expected_answer_contains: FlexPro drive operates from 65°C ambient
full_expected_answer: The FlexPro PCB drive operates from 0°C to 65°C ambient temperature. Storage temperature range is -40°C to +85°C. Exceeding the maximum ambient temperature requires derating the output current per the thermal derating curve. For PCB integration, use 1 oz copper planes and star-point grounding to minimize thermal resistance. For the full guide, download the AMC Hardware Manual — FlexPro PCB from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: How do I configure Integrator Decay Active Window?
expected_answer_contains: DriveWare, Active Window parameter define position
full_expected_answer: In DriveWare, set the Active Window parameter to define the position error threshold (in encoder counts) below which Integrator Decay is active. The decay rate is configured from 0–100%. Velocity and position integrators each have independent decay rates. A well-tuned Active Window prevents integrator wind-up at rest without affecting dynamic response. For the full guide, download the AMC Software Manual — DriveWare from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: What is the CRC algorithm used in AMC serial communication?
expected_answer_contains: RS485/RS232 serial communication uses CRC-16-CCITT XModem
full_expected_answer: AMC RS485/RS232 serial communication uses CRC-16-CCITT XModem for message integrity. The CRC is computed over the entire message (SOF + address + control + index + sub-index + data) and appended as the last 2 bytes (CRC-high, CRC-low). The XModem polynomial is 0x1021 with initial value 0x0000. Verify the CRC on every received message before processing. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 006 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How does the AMC EtherCAT ESI XML file work?
expected_answer_contains: (EtherCAT Slave Information) file describes drive's
full_expected_answer: The ESI (EtherCAT Slave Information) XML file describes the drive's PDO structure, object dictionary, and device identity to the EtherCAT master. When modifying PDO assignments (e.g., adding or removing objects), update the ESI XML to match the new configuration, then write it to the drive EEPROM using TwinCAT. Mismatches between the ESI XML and drive EEPROM configuration cause initialization errors. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 017 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I configure CANopen NMT state transitions?
expected_answer_contains: CANopen controls network state machine: Initialization
full_expected_answer: CANopen NMT controls the network state machine: Initialization → Pre-Operational → Operational → Stopped. From the master, send NMT commands to individual nodes (using Node-ID) or all nodes (Node-ID = 0). To start a node, send NMT Start Remote Node (01h). To enter Pre-Op, send 80h. Emergency objects are only transmitted in Operational state. Verify Node-ID and NMT state before attempting PDO communication. For the full guide, download the AMC Communication Manual — CANopen from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I configure RS485 baud rate and address on a DigiFlex drive?
expected_answer_contains: Default RS485 settings 9600 baud address
full_expected_answer: Default RS485 settings are 9600 baud and address 63. To change them, write new values to NVM via the serial interface or ACE software. RS232 and RS485 parameters are stored independently in NVM — changing RS232 settings does not affect RS485 settings. For multi-drop RS485 networks, assign unique addresses to each drive. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 002 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What is the AMC serial message header format?
expected_answer_contains: serial message header format byte A5h,
full_expected_answer: The AMC serial message header format is: SOF byte A5h, address byte, control byte, 2-byte index, 1-byte sub-index (offset), data word(s), and 2-byte CRC-16-CCITT XModem. The control byte encodes message type (read/write) and data length. Interface input commands use index 45h. All values are little-endian. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 036 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I configure CANopen PDO mapping?
expected_answer_contains: CANopen mapping configured writes communication (1400h–17FFh
full_expected_answer: CANopen PDO mapping is configured via SDO writes to the PDO communication (1400h–17FFh for RPDOs, 1800h–1BFFh for TPDOs) and mapping objects (1600h–17FFh, 1A00h–1BFFh). Disable the PDO (set number of mapped objects = 0) before modifying mapping entries. Each mapping entry is a 32-bit value encoding object index (16 bits), sub-index (8 bits), and bit length (8 bits). Re-enable the PDO after all mapping changes. For the full guide, download the AMC Communication Manual — CANopen from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What are the FlexPro PCB design requirements for I2C expander?
expected_answer_contains: FlexPro firmware versions prior 1-2021-4 require
full_expected_answer: FlexPro PCB firmware versions prior to 1-2021-4 require an I2C expander on the PCB design for proper I/O expansion. Firmware 1-2021-4 and later removed this requirement. If designing a PCB with older firmware in use, include the I2C expander circuit per the hardware manual. For new designs, update to firmware 1-2021-4 or later to eliminate this dependency. For the full guide, download the AMC Hardware Manual — FlexPro PCB from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: How do I configure the Waveform Generator in DriveWare?
expected_answer_contains: DriveWare Waveform Generator provides status indicator
full_expected_answer: The DriveWare Waveform Generator provides a status indicator showing the generator state (idle, running, fault). Configure waveform type (sine, square, triangle), amplitude, frequency, and offset. The generator can be used for testing current loop response, velocity loop bandwidth, and mechanical resonance. Monitor the real-time status indicator to confirm the generator is running before interpreting oscilloscope data. For the full guide, download the AMC Software Manual — DriveWare from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: What is the POWERLINK network specification for AMC drives?
expected_answer_contains: drives support POWERLINK Mbit/s with maximum
full_expected_answer: AMC drives support POWERLINK at 100 Mbit/s with a maximum of 240 nodes per network. POWERLINK uses a time-slot protocol managed by a Managing Node (MN), which polls Controlled Nodes (CN) in sequence for deterministic real-time performance. Cycle times of 200–400 µs are typical. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 001 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I configure the EtherCAT drive EEPROM for new PDO assignments?
expected_answer_contains: After modifying assignments DriveWare updating file,
full_expected_answer: After modifying PDO assignments in DriveWare and updating the ESI XML file, write the new configuration to the drive EEPROM using TwinCAT's EEPROM write function. In TwinCAT, select the EtherCAT device, navigate to the EEPROM tab, and write the updated ESI XML. The drive must be in pre-operational state during the EEPROM write. Incorrect EEPROM contents will prevent the slave from initializing. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 017 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What is the default IP address of the MACC controller?
expected_answer_contains: MACC default address 192.168.100.50. Configure your
full_expected_answer: The MACC default IP address is 192.168.100.50. Configure your PC to IP address 192.168.100.240 with subnet 255.255.255.0 to communicate with the MACC out of the box. Verify connectivity with a ping before attempting to connect with the C&M compiler. The C&M compiler target for MACC programs is MAL-MACC. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 047 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What is the over-temperature fault threshold on analog drives?
expected_answer_contains: over-temperature fault analog drives triggers when
full_expected_answer: The over-temperature fault on AMC analog drives triggers when the internal thermistor exceeds 65°C. The red LED illuminates and the bridge disables. Allow the drive to cool, verify adequate airflow, and check that the ambient temperature is within the 0–65°C operating range. Inspect mounting for proper thermal contact if using a heatsink. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 009 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What are the MACC C&M compiler targets?
expected_answer_contains: compiler targets MACC programs MAL-MACC (for
full_expected_answer: The C&M compiler targets for MACC programs are MAL-MACC (for the MACC motion controller). Compile your motion program in the C&M IDE selecting the MAL-MACC target, then download via Ethernet to the MACC at its default IP 192.168.100.50. Ensure the MACC firmware is up to date before downloading new programs. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 047 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I configure CANopen SDO expedited transfers?
expected_answer_contains: CANopen (Service Data Object) expedited transfers
full_expected_answer: CANopen SDO (Service Data Object) expedited transfers are used for configuration parameter access. An expedited write (download) sends the data in a single 8-byte CAN frame: command byte, index (2 bytes LE), sub-index (1 byte), and 4 bytes of data. Read (upload) uses the same frame structure. The default SDO server COB-ID is 600h + Node-ID (request) and 580h + Node-ID (response). Confirm the correct Node-ID before sending SDO commands. For the full guide, download the AMC Communication Manual — CANopen from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: What does the CONT. CURR. REDUCTION DIP switch do on analog drives?
expected_answer_contains: CONT. CURR. REDUCTION switch analog drives
full_expected_answer: The CONT. CURR. REDUCTION DIP switch on analog drives reduces the continuous current limit by a fixed percentage (typically 50%) when activated, providing additional thermal protection for low-duty-cycle applications. The CURRENT SCALING switch adjusts the overall current output scaling. Both switches work in conjunction with the CURR LIMIT potentiometer setting. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 011 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I configure the PLS time-based pulse mode?
expected_answer_contains: time-based mode, output pulse generated fixed
full_expected_answer: In time-based PLS mode, the output pulse is generated at a fixed time interval rather than based on axis position. Configure the pulse period and pulse width in the PLS parameter table. This mode is useful for generating encoder simulation outputs or trigger pulses for external equipment at a fixed rate. For the full guide, download the AMC Application Note 034 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I configure ACE PLS parameters?
expected_answer_contains: software parameters table includes: Lower Edge
full_expected_answer: The ACE software PLS parameters table includes: Lower Edge (position or time threshold for output activation), Upper Edge (deactivation threshold), Repeat Delta (increment for rotary rollover), repeat count, and output channel assignment. Configure in the PLS setup panel within ACE. Verify the rotary rollover condition (Lower Edge + Repeat Delta > Upper Edge) when using rotary mode. For the full guide, download the AMC Software Manual — ACE from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: What safety documentation is required for the STO function?
expected_answer_contains: function safety documentation requires: 62061 certification
full_expected_answer: The STO function safety documentation requires: IEC 62061 SIL 3 certification (dual-channel STO-1 and STO-2), ISO 13849-1 Category 4 / PL e certification, and a monthly proof test procedure to verify both channels operate independently. Retain the proof test records as part of your machine safety file. The hardware manual safety section (p.10) contains the full STO safety specification and installation requirements. For the full guide, download the AMC Hardware Manual — DigiFlex Panel EtherCAT from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What is the Ethernet/IP control state machine?
expected_answer_contains: Ethernet/IP drive implements state machine with
full_expected_answer: The Ethernet/IP drive implements the CiA 402 state machine with 7 states: Not Ready to Switch On, Switch On Disabled, Ready to Switch On, Switched On, Operation Enabled, Quick Stop Active, and Fault. Drive enable sequence: send controlword to transition Switch On Disabled → Ready to Switch On → Switched On → Operation Enabled. To clear a fault, send a Fault Reset command (controlword bit 7 rising edge) from the Fault state. Eight operating modes are available via assembly instance 913. For the full guide, download the AMC Communication Manual — EtherNet/IP FlexPro from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: How do I configure the velocity command scaling?
expected_answer_contains: velocity scaling units configure communication channel
full_expected_answer: Set the velocity scaling units and configure the communication channel parameters. The scaling determines how network velocity commands are translated to motor speed. For the full guide, download "AMC_SW_Manual_ACE.pdf" from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: What do the LED indicators mean on a DigiFlex drive?
expected_answer_contains: DigiFlex drives have LEDs: **Power LED**
full_expected_answer: DigiFlex drives have two LEDs: **Power LED** — GREEN = power OK, RED = shunt regulator active (excess energy during regeneration), RED/GREEN flashing = shunt cycling on/off. **Status LED** — GREEN = power bridge enabled (drive running), RED = power bridge disabled (via inhibit input or fault condition). If both LEDs are off, check DC supply voltage and fuse. If Status LED is RED, check: (1) inhibit inputs are in the enabled state, (2) no active fault codes in DriveWare status panel, (3) STO inputs are not active. For more details, download the DigiFlex HW Installation Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What do the LED indicators mean on a FlexPro drive?
expected_answer_contains: FlexPro drives have Status LEDs that
full_expected_answer: FlexPro drives have Status LEDs that indicate: **GREEN solid** = drive enabled and operating normally. **RED solid** = fault condition or bridge disabled. **GREEN blinking** = drive powered but not enabled (waiting for enable command). **RED blinking** = specific fault active. Check ACE software for detailed fault codes. If no LEDs are lit, verify DC supply voltage is within the 10-55 VDC range and check the power connector. For more details, download the FlexPro PCB HW Installation Manual from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What do the LED indicators mean on an AxCent drive?
expected_answer_contains: AxCent drives have single status LED:
full_expected_answer: AxCent drives have a single status LED: **GREEN** = drive enabled, normal operation. **RED** = fault condition — the drive has disabled its output. Common causes: over-temperature (>65°C internal), over-voltage on DC bus, invalid Hall sensor state, or short circuit. If the LED turns RED during motor rotation, check Hall sensor wiring and phasing. The LED should stay GREEN through a full shaft revolution. If it flashes RED at certain positions, the Hall sensor sequence is incorrect. For more details and the 9-step troubleshooting procedure, download App Note 009 from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What does a red LED mean on an analog drive?
expected_answer_contains: analog drive indicates fault condition. Follow
full_expected_answer: A red LED on an AMC analog drive indicates a fault condition. Follow the 9-step isolation procedure from App Note 009: (1) Remove motor connections — if LED turns GREEN, the fault is motor-related. (2) Check Hall sensors — valid states are 60° apart (001, 010, 011, 100, 101, 110). Invalid states 000 or 111 cause a fault. (3) Check for short circuits between motor phases. (4) Verify DC bus voltage is within range. (5) Check inhibit/enable inputs. (6) Verify current limit DIP switches are set correctly. (7) Check for over-temperature. (8) Reduce command signal to zero and re-enable. (9) If still RED, the drive may need replacement. Download App Note 009 (Red LED Troubleshooting) from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What causes an over-voltage fault?
expected_answer_contains: over-voltage fault occurs when voltage exceeds
full_expected_answer: An over-voltage fault occurs when the DC bus voltage exceeds the drive's maximum rating. Common causes: (1) **Regeneration** — the motor acts as a generator during deceleration, pumping energy back into the DC bus. Solution: use a shunt resistor/regulator, reduce deceleration rate, or add a larger bus capacitor. (2) **Power supply voltage too high** — verify supply is within the drive's rated range. (3) **Back-EMF from large motors** — high-speed motors can generate voltage that exceeds the bus limit during braking. The drive will auto-disable and the Status LED turns RED. Clear the fault by cycling power or sending a fault reset command (Controlword bit 7). For shunt resistor sizing, see App Note 018 (Power Supply Sizing) at a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What causes an over-current fault?
expected_answer_contains: over-current fault occurs when output current
full_expected_answer: An over-current fault occurs when output current exceeds the drive's peak rating. Common causes: (1) **Short circuit** between motor phases or phase to ground — check wiring with a multimeter. (2) **Incorrect current loop tuning** — gains too high cause oscillation and current spikes. Reduce Kp first. (3) **Motor stall** — motor physically blocked causes current to rise to the limit. (4) **Incorrect motor parameters** — wrong Ke (back-EMF constant) or resistance values in drive configuration. (5) **Incorrect commutation phasing** — causes the drive to fight the motor instead of driving it. Fix: run auto-commutation in ACE/DriveWare. The drive disables output and Status LED turns RED. For tuning help, see App Note 015 (Current Loop Tuning) at a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What causes an over-temperature fault?
expected_answer_contains: over-temperature fault triggers when drive's internal
full_expected_answer: An over-temperature fault triggers when the drive's internal temperature exceeds its thermal limit (~65°C for analog drives, varies by model for digital). Common causes: (1) **Insufficient heatsinking** — the drive needs proper thermal contact with a heatsink or chassis. Check thermal pad/compound. (2) **Excessive continuous current** — running near peak current for extended periods. Reduce load or use a higher-rated drive. (3) **Poor airflow** — ensure adequate ventilation around the drive. (4) **High ambient temperature** — derate the drive's current capacity at elevated temperatures (check datasheet). The drive will auto-disable. It will NOT re-enable until temperature drops below the threshold. For mounting and thermal guidelines, see the HW Installation Manual for your drive family at a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: Why is my motor running rough or jerky?
expected_answer_contains: Rough jerky motor motion usually caused
full_expected_answer: Rough or jerky motor motion is usually caused by: (1) **Poor current loop tuning** — the most common cause. Run auto-tune in ACE (Tuning tab → Auto-Tune) or manually adjust current loop Kp/Ki. Start with low gains and increase gradually. Target bandwidth: 1-3 kHz. (2) **Incorrect commutation** — if the motor jumps or vibrates at standstill, run auto-commutation to correct the electrical angle offset. (3) **Encoder noise** — check encoder wiring for proper shielding and grounding. Differential signals (A+/A-, B+/B-) should be used. (4) **Velocity loop too aggressive** — reduce velocity Kp gain. Target bandwidth: 50-200 Hz. (5) **Mechanical issues** — coupling misalignment, bearing wear, or excessive friction. (6) **Incorrect pole count** — verify motor pole pairs match drive configuration. See App Note 015 (Current Loop Tuning) at a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: My motor won't spin. What should I check?
expected_answer_contains: motor won't spin, check this order:
full_expected_answer: If the motor won't spin, check in this order: (1) **Status LED** — is it GREEN? If RED, there's a fault (see LED troubleshooting). (2) **Enable inputs** — all inhibit/enable inputs must be in the correct state. Check hardware enable pin and software enable (Controlword). (3) **Command signal** — verify the drive is receiving a non-zero command. Check analog input voltage or network command value. (4) **Motor connections** — verify all phase wires (U/V/W or A/B/C) are connected. Check continuity. (5) **Feedback** — encoder or Hall sensors must be connected and returning valid signals. Check in ACE/DriveWare scope. (6) **Current limits** — check that peak/continuous current limits aren't set to zero. (7) **STO (Safe Torque Off)** — if equipped, both STO inputs must be HIGH (+24V) to enable. (8) **Operating mode** — ensure the drive is in the correct mode (position/velocity/current). For step-by-step commissioning, see the ACE Quick Reference Guide at a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Software Downloads.

question: I can't communicate with my drive. What should I check?
expected_answer_contains: can't connect your drive RS-232: **Check
full_expected_answer: If you can't connect to your drive via USB or RS-232: (1) **Check cable** — USB: use a standard USB-A to USB-B cable. RS-232: verify pinout (TX/RX may need to be swapped). (2) **Check port** — in ACE/DriveWare, select the correct COM port. Try different ports. (3) **Check address** — default address is 63 for FlexPro (ACE) and varies for DigiFlex (DriveWare). (4) **Check baud rate** — default is 9600 for RS-232 on DigiFlex. (5) **Driver issues** — on Windows, check Device Manager for the USB driver. Try uninstalling and re-enumerating. (6) **Power** — the drive must be powered on for communication. (7) **Multi-axis** — if multiple drives on RS-485 bus, each must have a unique address. (8) **Termination** — RS-485 bus needs 120Ω termination at each end. See App Note 050 (USB & RS232 Troubleshooting) at a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: My EtherCAT drive is not connecting to the master.
expected_answer_contains: your EtherCAT drive won't connect: **Check
full_expected_answer: If your EtherCAT drive won't connect: (1) **Check cables** — use Cat5e or better Ethernet cables. EtherCAT is daisy-chained: Master OUT → Drive 1 IN, Drive 1 OUT → Drive 2 IN, etc. (2) **Check ESI/XML file** — your EtherCAT master needs the correct XML device description file. Download from a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Device Description Files. (3) **Check network topology** — verify IN/OUT port connections are correct (not reversed). (4) **Check DC sync** — if using Cyclic Synchronous modes, distributed clocks must be configured in the master. (5) **Check state machine** — the drive should progress through Init → Pre-Op → Safe-Op → Op. If stuck, check the master's error log. (6) **Check firmware** — older firmware may not support your master's EtherCAT features. Update via ACE/DriveWare. For detailed setup, see the EtherCAT Communication Manual at a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Communication Manuals.

question: My CANopen drive is not communicating on the bus.
expected_answer_contains: your CANopen drive won't communicate: **Check
full_expected_answer: If your CANopen drive won't communicate: (1) **Check wiring** — CAN_H, CAN_L, and GND must be connected. Use twisted pair cable. (2) **Termination** — 120Ω resistor required at EACH END of the bus (not in the middle). Measure ~60Ω between CAN_H and CAN_L with power off. (3) **Baud rate** — all devices must use the same baud rate. Default is 250 kbit/s for DigiFlex, configurable in DriveWare. (4) **Node ID** — each device must have a unique node ID (1-127). Default varies by drive. (5) **Bus length** — maximum depends on baud rate: 1 Mbit/s = 25m, 500k = 100m, 250k = 250m, 125k = 500m. (6) **Stub length** — keep drop lines under 0.3m at 1 Mbit/s. (7) **NMT state** — send NMT Start Remote Node to transition drive to Operational state. For bus cabling guide, see App Note 005 at a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: What is STO and how do I wire it?
expected_answer_contains: (Safe Torque Off) safety function that
full_expected_answer: STO (Safe Torque Off) is a safety function that removes torque from the motor without removing power to the drive. It's certified to IEC 62061 SIL 3 / ISO 13849 Category 4 PL e. **Wiring:** STO has two independent channels (STO-1 and STO-2). Both must be HIGH (+24V) to enable the drive. When either goes LOW, the drive immediately removes motor current. **Common issue:** If your drive won't enable and you see an STO fault, check that BOTH STO inputs have +24V applied. On FlexPro PCB drives, STO pins are on connector P1. On DigiFlex Panel drives, STO is on the I/O connector. **Testing:** STO must be tested periodically per your machine's safety requirements. Remove +24V from one channel at a time — the motor should stop immediately. For full STO wiring diagrams and certification details, see the HW Installation Manual for your drive at a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Hardware Manuals.

question: What is the shunt regulator and when do I need an external shunt resistor?
expected_answer_contains: **shunt regulator** protects drive from over-voltage
full_expected_answer: The **shunt regulator** protects the drive from over-voltage during regeneration (when the motor decelerates and pumps energy back). Most DigiFlex and FlexPro drives have a built-in shunt regulator that can dissipate small amounts of regenerative energy. **When you need an external shunt resistor:** (1) High-inertia loads with fast deceleration. (2) Vertical axes where gravity constantly regenerates. (3) If you see frequent over-voltage faults or the Power LED flashes RED/GREEN. **Sizing:** Calculate regenerative energy = ½ × J × (ω₁² - ω₂²) where J = total inertia and ω = speed in rad/s. The shunt resistor must dissipate this energy within the deceleration time. See App Note 018 (Power Supply Sizing) and App Note 058 (Inrush Current) at a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.

question: How do I troubleshoot encoder problems?
expected_answer_contains: Common encoder issues: **No position feedback**
full_expected_answer: Common encoder issues: (1) **No position feedback** — check encoder cable connections, verify +5V encoder supply is present, check for cable damage. (2) **Position jumps or noise** — use differential signals (A+/A-, B+/B-) with shielded cable. Ground the shield at the drive end only. (3) **Wrong direction** — swap A and B channels, or change direction parameter in ACE/DriveWare. (4) **Position runaway** — encoder resolution (counts/rev) doesn't match drive configuration. Verify in ACE Parameter Tree → Feedback. (5) **Index pulse issues** — ensure Z/Index channel is connected if using homing to index. (6) **Absolute encoder faults** — check fault word at object 2027.04h (EtherCAT/CANopen). Hiperface codes: 01h-28h indicate specific sensor errors. (7) **Hall sensor issues** — valid Hall states cycle through 6 unique patterns. State 000 or 111 = fault (broken wire or sensor). See App Note 040 (Absolute Feedback Configuration) at a-m-c.com > Support > Downloads > Application Notes.
